title: The Chronicles of Narnia — Creation, Fall, and Redemption as Tattva Cycles
subtitle: The creation of Narnia as the emanation of the tattvas, the White Witch as Māyā freezing consciousness, Aslan as the daimon at the threshold, each book as a different tattva-range

summary.one_line: Lewis's Chronicles mapped onto the Śaiva emanation scheme: the creation of Narnia as the descent of the tattvas from pure to impure, the White Witch as Māyā freezing consciousness into the contract...
summary.scope: Covers the seven Chronicles of Narnia, with primary focus on The Magician's Nephew (creation), The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe (incarnation/sacrifice), The Voyage of the Dawn Treader (journey/transformation), and The Last Battle (dissolution). Secondary treatment of the remaining three books.

sources.label: Lewis, C.S. — The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe (1950)
Lewis, C.S. — The Voyage of the Dawn Treader (1952)
Lewis, C.S. — The Horse and His Boy (1954)

body.text: [See Narnia Magician's Nephew ch.8-10. The creation of Narnia is the emanation of the tattvas from the pure to the impure. Aslan sings the world into being — not a past event but a present act, a song that begins in darkness (the pure, undifferentiated para level) and rises through layers of increasing form. First the stars (the first tattvas: śiva, śakti, sadāśiva, īśvara, śuddhavidyā), then the sun (the subtle instruments), then the earth (the elements), then the animals (the embodied souls). The song is the śabda-brahman — the Word-as-vibration that is the substrate of all manifestation. Digory and Polly witness the tattvas emerging from the naught: first as light, then as colour, then as shape, then as life. The sequence is the exact descent of the 36 tattvas from parāśiva to pṛthivī.]

[See Narnia Lion/Witch/Wardrobe and Magician's Nephew. The White Witch Jadis is Māyā — the power that freezes consciousness into the contracted state. In Magician's Nephew she is introduced eating a golden apple (the object of desire that binds the soul to the wheel), and her magic is the power to make things 'always winter but never Christmas' — the perpetual contraction of the life-force into the sheaths of limitation. In Lion/Witch/Wardrobe, she turns Narnians to stone: literalising the process of māyā whereby consciousness is captured in the tamas quality, frozen into the inert. The Turkish Delight she feeds Edmund is the sweetened poison of the world of objects — the taste that makes the soul forget its origin. Her deep magic from the dawn of time is the law of karmic causation; her deeper magic is the power that breaks it.]

[See throughout Narnia, especially Lion/Witch/Wardrobe. Aslan is the daimon who appears at the threshold — neither fully within the world nor fully beyond it. He is not a tame lion ('of course he isn't safe — but he's good'). The daimon is not safe because it does not obey the ego's categories; it arrives when it chooses, speaks what must be heard, and disappears when the soul tries to grasp it. Aslan appears at the decisive thresholds: the Stone Table (sacrifice), the end of the world (transfiguration), the stable door (final judgment). He is the function, not the being — the presence that appears when the soul reaches its limit and something other-than-itself must break through. His death and resurrection is the daimonic pattern: the guide who descends with the soul into the underworld and returns to show the way out.]

[See Narnia Lion/Witch/Wardrobe ch.1-2. The wardrobe is the doorway between worlds — the physical threshold that opens onto the subtle plane. It functions at the madhyama level: the intermediate state where the vaikharī (fully articulated, physical) world of the spare room touches the para (transcendent) world of Narnia. The wardrobe is not a symbol; it is a literal passage that operates by the logic of the subtle body. Fur coats give way to snow — the physical transitions to the subtle without the subject's conscious preparation. Lucy enters first because children (souls not yet fully hardened into the adult ego) have easier access. The wardrobe is the daimonic track: the opening that appears when attention is directed away from the self and toward the threshold. Later books use other doors (a painting, a door in the air, a stable), each representing a different level of access to the subtle.]

[See Narnia Dawn Treader throughout. The voyage of the Dawn Treader is the journey through the tattva-ranges — the progressive purification of the soul as it moves from the gross to the subtle. Each island is a tattva-level: the Lone Islands (gross, physical — the pṛthivī/ap/tejas level), Dragon Island (the purification of vāyu — Eustace's dragon-form shed like a sheath), Deathwater Island (the fixity of desire — the ākāśa where gold-fixation crystallises), the Island of the Voices (the manas level — the invisible Dufflepuds whose bodies are hidden), Dark Island (the ahaṃkāra — the fear that arises from the I-sense), and the Island of the Star (the buddhi — the awakened star whose song is the discernment of the real). The journey ends at the Lamb at the end of the world — the return to the pure īśvara level, the source from which the tattvas first emanated.]

[See Narnia Last Battle throughout. The Last Battle is the dissolution (pralaya) of one cycle of manifestation — the withdrawal of the tattvas back into their source. The false Aslan (the māyā covering of the real) is worshipped by a clever ape and a gullible donkey: the intellect (buddhi) tricked by appearance, the ego (ahaṃkāra) clinging to a comforting image. The dwarfs who cannot see the real Aslan even when he stands before them are the souls so contracted by the kañcukas that they cannot perceive liberation when it arrives — 'the Dwarfs are for the Dwarfs.' The stable door is the final threshold: the door that leads, paradoxically, inward to the 'true Narnia' — the transcendent archetype of the world that has dissolved. Aslan's final words — 'further up and further in' — are the perpetual invitation of the daimon to move beyond every achieved level, to never mistake the current tattva for the final one.]

gaps: Detailed tattva-by-tattva mapping of the creation sequence needed
Deeper analysis of the white witch as both maya and kala needed
Each book's doorway mechanism could be a separate passage
Island-by-island exegesis linking each to a specific tattva needed
The true Narnia as the suddha-tattva realm could be expanded

issues.title: All passages are meta-commentary — need primary text extraction
issues.description: Passages currently describe the esoteric mapping. Real passages should include actual quotations from the novels with analysis.

issues.title: The Wood Between the Worlds as the unmanifest substrate
issues.description: The Wood Between the Worlds in Magician's Nephew is the state between manifestations — the level of pure potentiality where all worlds are equally accessible. This maps onto the śūnya (the void) or the anāśrita (the unsupported) of the Śaiva system. Not yet developed.

versions.summary: Initial RO created mapping Narnia onto the Śaiva tattva-emanation scheme. Six passages covering creation as tattva descent, the White Witch as Māyā, Aslan as daimon, the wardrobe as madhyama doorway, the Dawn Treader as tattva-journey, and the Last Battle as pralaya.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 7 primary sources and 6 passages
